coredata.dc:title: Unveiling a novel nanozyme cofactor: a highly activated Fe-CDs-derived colorimetric sensor array for comprehensive authentication of diverse tea products

coredata.dc:description: Tea has antioxidant, anti-aging, hypotensive, and other functions. The tea polyphenol profile is of great significance for the identification and quality control of diverse tea products. Herein, an iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) with peroxidase-like activity was synthesized to construct a colorimetric sensor array for the tea polyphenols analysis in a complex matrix. Unexpectedly, it is found that the addition of tea polyphenols leads to an admirable increase in the activity of Fe-CDs, indicating the role of tea polyphenols as a cofactor of Fe-CDs nanozyme. The exploration based on multiple techniques reveals that the polyphenols can absorb on the Fe-CDs surface to form the new complex and effectively enhance the catalytic performance of Fe-CDs. This enhancement effect is closely related to the elemental doping, surface condition of nanozyme, and the polyphenol concentration. Taking TMB as the substrate, the colorimetric fingerprint generated in the presence of different tea polyphenols are extracted from the sensing array. The results of principal component analysis (PCA) and hierarchical clustering analysis (HCA) show that five tea polyphenols of different types, concentrations, and tea polyphenol mixtures of different proportions are successfully distinguished through pattern recognition methods. Moreover, the identification of tea leaves from different years and types, as well as various tea drinks, has been further realized. This study not only paves a new direction for enhancing the nanozyme activity, but provides an effective and convenient strategy for the quality control of tea products.

(C) UV–vis and normalized fluorescence spectra of Fe-CDs. (D) FTIR spectra of CDs and Fe-CDs. (E) XRD pattern and (F) XPS survey scan of Fe-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (G) Fe 2p, (H) C 1s, and (I) N 1s.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of different reaction systems. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB-H2O2-Fe-CDs system before and after adding different polyphenols. The Lineweaver-Burk activity of Fe-CDs and Fe-CDs + EC for (C) TMB and (D) H2O2. The Lineweaver-Burk plots of (E) various TMB concentrations and (F) various H2O2 concentrations under three different dosages of H2O2 and TMB.

(A) ESR spectra of Fe-CDs. (B) The UV–vis absorbance at 650 nm of the Fe-CDs/TMB/H2O2 system before and after the addition of radical scavengers. The fluorescence emission spectra of different free radical scavengers in the presence of Fe-CDs: (C) PTA, (D) ABDA, (E) DHE. (F) Fluorescence emission spectra of Fe-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of EC. (G) Fluorescence lifetime images of Fe-CDs before and after the addition of EC. (H) The mechanism of TMB oxidation by Fe-CDs with polyphenols as cofactors. (I) The UV–vis absorbance at 652 nm of the Nanozyme/TMB/H2O2 system before and after the addition of EC. (J) Absorbance trend map of the system at 650 nm in the presence of different concentrations of EC.

(A) Radar plot of response (ΔA370, ΔA450, and ΔA650) patterns from five various tea polyphenols of 50 μg/mL. PCA score plots for discriminating five tea polyphenols of (B) 100 μg/mL, (C) 10 μg/mL, (D) 1 μg/mL. (E) Heat map of the sensor array in response to five tea polyphenols of 10 μg/mL. (F) HCA dendrogram for five various tea polyphenols of 50 μg/mL. PCA score plots for discriminating mixtures of (G) binary, (H) ternary and (I) quinary polyphenols. (J) Radar plot of the sensor array for different channels used for the differentiation of quinary polyphenol mixtures. (K) PCA score plots and (L) the linear regression of the first discriminant factor versus the negative logarithm of different amounts of EC.

head.ce:title: Unveiling a novel nanozyme cofactor: a highly activated Fe-CDs-derived colorimetric sensor array for comprehensive authentication of diverse tea products

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tea has antioxidant, anti-aging, hypotensive, and other functions. The tea polyphenol profile is of great significance for the identification and quality control of diverse tea products. Herein, an iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) with peroxidase-like activity was synthesized to construct a colorimetric sensor array for the tea polyphenols analysis in a complex matrix. Unexpectedly, it is found that the addition of tea polyphenols leads to an admirable increase in the activity of Fe-CDs, indicating the role of tea polyphenols as a cofactor of Fe-CDs nanozyme. The exploration based on multiple techniques reveals that the polyphenols can absorb on the Fe-CDs surface to form the new complex and effectively enhance the catalytic performance of Fe-CDs. This enhancement effect is closely related to the elemental doping, surface condition of nanozyme, and the polyphenol concentration. Taking TMB as the substrate, the colorimetric fingerprint generated in the presence of different tea polyphenols are extracted from the sensing array. The results of principal component analysis (PCA) and hierarchical clustering analysis (HCA) show that five tea polyphenols of different types, concentrations, and tea polyphenol mixtures of different proportions are successfully distinguished through pattern recognition methods. Moreover, the identification of tea leaves from different years and types, as well as various tea drinks, has been further realized. This study not only paves a new direction for enhancing the nanozyme activity, but provides an effective and convenient strategy for the quality control of tea products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tea is one of the most popular beverages in the world. It has various biological functions such as antioxidant, anti-aging, hypotensive, tumor inhibition, and prevention of cardiovascular diseases (Yan et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2024). The tea polyphenolic compounds with similar structures and properties are the main active ingredients in tea (Jia et al., 2021). The tea polyphenols profile varies according to the different tea types and quality, and is an important indicator for the comprehensive authentication of diverse tea products. Therefore, the detection of polyphenols in tea is of great importance for the identification and quality control of tea. So far, many strategies have been proposed for the detection of tea polyphenols, such as high performance liquid chromatography (Nian et al., 2019), liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (García-Villalba et al., 2017), electrochemistry (Kou et al., 2014), and flow injection analysis (Franco-Ulloa et al., 2024). Among these methods, some of them are limited by the complicated sample pretreatment processes, high expense, and the need of professional operation, while others are circumscribed by the inability of simultaneous detection of multiple similar targets, and the interference from real samples. Thus, there is an urgent and practical demand to develop a convenient and economical method for the rapid and accurate analysis of multiple polyphenol active substances in tea.

The sensor array technology shows great potential for the high-throughput detection of multiple analytes. Equipped with a series of cross-reactive non-specific recognition elements, the sensor array will generate the complex and unique fingerprint response for each analyte (Jing et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2024). These characteristic responses can be further distinguished through other multivariate statistical methods such as principal component analysis (PCA) and hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) (Guo et al., 2025), providing a general method for the simultaneous identification of multiple analytes in complex systems (Wu et al., 2023). The core component of the sensor array is the recognition elements, which decide the sensitivity of the overall analysis. Nanozyme, the potential replacement of natural enzyme (Ren et al., 2025; Singh, 2024), is newly emerged as recognition or sensing elements in sensor arrays due to their adjustable catalytic activity (Zhu et al., 2025), ease of synthesis (Hou et al., 2024), and high stability (Liu et al., 2024). Nowadays, nanozyme-based sensor arrays have shown wide applications in the fields of disease diagnosis (Zhou et al., 2023a), food safety (Huang et al., 2025b; Song et al., 2023), and environmental monitoring (Li et al., 2023). So far, a variety of nanozymes have been developed, including precious metals (Sang et al., 2024) and metal oxides (Liu et al., 2021), carbon-based nanomaterials (Sun et al., 2018), metal-organic frameworks (MOF) (Ma et al., 2024), and even polystyrene nanoparticles (Huang et al., 2024).

Carbon dots (CDs), a zero-dimensional nanomaterial, is a star member of carbon nanomaterials due to its excellent properties of low toxicity, tunable size, admirable electronic properties, rich surface functional groups (Li et al., 2024a), etc. CDs have now been extensively applied across different fields, such as biosensing (Yan et al., 2024a), catalysis (Kim et al., 2024), antibacterial (Yu et al., 2024), and bioimaging (Zhou et al., 2023b). The newly emerged function of CDs is acting as nanozyme, which have a peroxidase-like activity to conduct catalytic reactions (Wareing et al., 2021; Zheng et al., 2015). Plenty of attention has been focused on improving the enzyme catalytic activity of carbon-based nanozyme, for example, surface modification or structure tuning (Meng et al., 2024), non-metal or metal element doping (Xiao et al., 2024), and synthesis of single-atom nanozyme (Shi et al., 2024). The role of cofactors, however, is often overlooked. Cofactors of natural enzymes are involved in a variety of biological processes, and the addition of cofactors can significantly affect the catalysis of natural enzymes, including enhancing catalytic activity, and increasing reaction specificity (Sun et al., 2023). Similarly, nanozyme may also have its own cofactor. For example, fluoride ion is used as an activator cofactor for Ce-MOF nanozymes by enhancing the oxygen vacancies related cerium redox cycle (Huang et al., 2025a). In addition, the presence of adenosine triphosphate disodium salt (ATP) can greatly enhance the peroxidase-like activity of AuNP (Shah et al., 2015; Vallabani et al., 2017). However, for CDs nanozymes, there are few reports on related cofactors.

In this work, we prepared the iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) for the recognition element in the sensor array, and surprisingly found that tea polyphenols can play as the nanozyme cofactor and effectively enhance the activity of Fe-CDs. As shown in Scheme 1 , the nanozyme converted the colorless chromogenic substrate 3, 3′,5, 5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into the blue oxidized TMB (ox-TMB) with the presence of H2O2, which exhibited obvious peroxidase-mimic activity. The addition of different polyphenols exerted varied enhancing effects on the enzyme activity, leading to distinct colorimetric responses. Three characteristic absorption peaks, as well as different reaction conditions, were used as the sensing channels to construct the sensing array. Five representative polyphenols were initially distinguished successfully. Based on this, binary, ternary, and quinary mixtures were discriminated, separately. The linear detection of specific polyphenol was also achieved. Furthermore, the obtained colorimetric sensor effectively discriminated ten commercial tea samples of different storage times, brands, and types, and even four tea beverages. Thanks to the high activity of Fe-CDs induced by polyphenol, the proposed sensor array rises as a convenient and powerful tool for the analysis of diverse tea products and also opens up new avenues for geo-authenticity identification and quality control in the tea industry.

0.7261 g FeCl3•6H2O, 0.5 g C10H14N2O8Na2 and 0.5 g C2H7NS·HCl were thoroughly dissolved in 25 mL water and then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and dialyzed for 12 h (500 Da). The solution was then freeze-dried to powder and stored at 4 °C for later use. The preparation of undoped CDs followed the same procedure just without the addition of FeCl3•6H2O.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CDs was studied with TMB and H2O2 as the substrates. 0.1 mL of TMB (5 mM), 0.1 mL of H2O2 (10 mM) and 0.1 mL of Fe-CDs (0.50 mg/mL) were added to 0.7 mL acetate buffer solution (0.2 M, pH 4.0). After incubation for 10 min at room temperature, the absorption at 650 nm of the mixture was measured using a UV–visible spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the colorimetric sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: 100 μL TMB (5 mM), 100 μL H2O2 (10 mM) and 100 μL Fe-CDs (0.50 mg/mL) were added to acetate buffer solution (0.2 M, pH 4.0) to obtain the final volume of 1 mL, noted as the blank group. For the experimental group, 100 μL of different target solutions were added into the blank solution and also enabled the final volume of 1 mL. After incubation at room temperature for 10 min, the absorption spectra were measured. The peak changes at 370 nm, 450 nm, and 650 nm (ΔA370, ΔA450, and ΔA650) of the blank group and the experimental group were applied as three recognition channels. In some complicated cases, the reaction pH and TMB concentration were also tuned and involved as other recognition channels. The experiment of each channel was repeated six times, and finally a data matrix of target number × channel number × 6 replicates was obtained and analyzed by PCA and HCA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of polyphenols and their multi-component mixtures
ce:section.ce:para: Five main active tea polyphenols, epicatechin (EC), epigallocatechin (EGC), catechin gallate (CG), gallic acid (GA), kaempferol 3-0 glucose rhamnoside (K), were studied as representative. 100 μL of the five polyphenols were added separately into the blank solution, following the same procedure in Section 2.3. Three channels of ΔA370, ΔA450 and ΔA650 were applied. Five concentrations from 100 μg/mL to 1 μg/mL were studied, and the test of each concentration produced a data matrix of 5 polyphenols × 3 channels × 6 replicates, which were subjected to PCA and HCA.

For the multi-component mixtures, the polyphenols were mixed in different concentration proportions with a total of 50 μg/mL. The binary mixture was prepared by mixing EC and EGC in proportions of 1:0, 0:1, 1:9, 3:7, 7:3, 4:1. The ternary mixture was a mixture of EC, EGC, and K polyphenols in proportions of 1:1:2, 1:3:6, 2:1:2, 2:1:1. The quinary mixture was EC, EGC, GA, CG, and K integrating with proportions of 1:1:1:1:1, 10:7:2:6:3, 5:8:10:9:6, 15:1:12:8:4, 9:8:10:12:7. The binary and ternary mixture followed the same procedure as separate polyphenols, including forming a data matrix of mixture number × 3 channels × 6 replicates. However, for the quinary mixture, besides the aforementioned 3 channels, the conditions of pH, H2O2, and TMB concentration were also involved in constructing the array, resulting in a bigger data matrix.

Different tea leaves and tea beverages were used as real tea products. The tea leaf samples were treated in two different ways: methanol extraction (a laboratory technique) and hot water brewing (simulating daily tea preparation), aiming to test whether the proposed array can identify tea samples across multiple scenarios.

The Guangxi Liupao tea of different years was tea leaves stored for 15 years, 10 years, 8 years and <1 year from two different manufacturers (Maosheng and Tianyu). The tea leaves were ground into powder with a mortar and sieved with a 100-mesh sieve. 20 mg of tea powder was mixed with 1 mL of 25 % methanol (preheated at 70 °C) and stirred at 70 °C for 15 min. Cooled to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min and the supernatant was taken as the solution for test.

Different brands of tea leaves, including Puer tea (PET), Liupao tea (LPT), Rose black tea (RBT), and Enshi Yulu (ESYL), were used to brew tea solutions. 0.2 g tea leave was steeped in 10 mL pure water at 80 °C. After soaking for 10 min, the tea samples were obtained by filtering the tea solution.

The tea beverages for discrimination were Ganpu tea (GPT), black oolong tea (BOT), black tea (BT) of Oriental Leaves, and lemon tea (LMT) of C'estbon. All of them were purchased from the supermarket (Guilin, China) and tested directly without treatment.

The obtained Fe-CDs was investigated by different techniques to confirm the successful synthesis. The TEM results showed that the Fe-CDs were well-dispersed spherical nanoparticles with an average size of about 3 nm (Fig. 1 A). The lattice spacing of 0.19 nm was observed in Fig. 1B, which is close to the (100)-plane spacing of graphene, indicating a carbon core structure (Liu et al., 2022c) and good crystallinity of Fe-CDs (Tan et al., 2017). The optical properties of Fe-CDs were studied via UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 1C). The obvious absorption peak at 252 nm was attributed to the n-π∗ transition of C=O. The Fe-CDs also showed the photoluminescence property, which was not excitation-dependent, with the optimal excited wavelength at 377 nm and emission at 441 nm. The surface functional groups of Fe-CDs and undoped CDs were explored by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). As displayed in Fig. 1D, the peaks at 3437 cm−1 and 1399 cm−1 corresponded to stretching -OH, and the peak at 2843 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of -CH. The strong peak at 1631 cm−1 indicated that the surface of Fe-CDs and CDs contained C=O, and 1091 cm−1 was the stretching vibration peak of C-O (Yang et al., 2023). Overall, the FTIR spectrum of Fe-CDs was basically the same as that of CDs, only with the stronger peak intensity, and this is consistent with the report that the doping of Fe element is favorable to introduce abundant oxygen-containing groups, i.e., -OH, C=O and C-O (Zhuo et al., 2019). As a result, the synthesized Fe-CDs have good water solubility.

For the crystal structure of Fe-CDs, the XRD pattern (Fig. 1E) showed a broad peak at about 26°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite (Liu et al., 2022a). The absence of the characteristic peak of Fe element was ascribed to its low doped content. The elemental composition of Fe-CDs was further analyzed by XPS. The Fe 2p characteristic peak was observed in the survey spectrum (Fig. 1F), demonstrating the Fe element was successfully doped. The high-resolution result in Fig. 1G showed that the Fe element on the surface of CDs existed in the form of Fe2+ and Fe3+, respectively, and the appearance of Fe2+ could be attributed to the reduction of some of the trivalent iron to divalent iron during the synthesis process. The C 1s peak was deconvoluted into three peaks (Fig. 1H), corresponding to the chemical bonds of O-C=O (288.3 eV), C=O (286.2 eV), and C=C/C-C (284.8 eV). The high-resolution spectra of N 1s was shown in Fig. 1I, where the peak at 400.07 eV corresponds to N-H, and the peak at 401.95 eV corresponds to the C-N peak.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CDs and its kinetic study
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase (POD)-like activity of Fe-CDs and CDs were explored in HAc-NaAc buffer solution at pH 4 with TMB as the chromogenic substrate. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the H2O2-TMB solution had almost no absorption, illustrating that the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 in the absence of a catalyst was very weak. Meanwhile, nearly no absorption peaks were observed when Fe-CDs were mixed with H2O2 and TMB, respectively. This implied Fe-CDs itself had negligible absorption in the range from 350 to 800 nm, and Fe-CDs had no oxidase-mimic activity. Only when Fe-CDs, H2O2, and TMB were present at the same time, the solution turned bright blue due to the production of ox-TMB, and significant absorption peaks appeared at 370 nm, 450 nm, and 650 nm. For comparison, the mixed systems of CDs-TMB and CDs-H2O2-TMB both exhibited very weak absorption. At present, some of the undoped CDs were reported to have certain POD-mimic activity (Song et al., 2020). However, the POD-like performance of the undoped CDs synthesized in this paper is low, which may be ascribed to the different raw materials and synthesis conditions. These obtained results demonstrated that Fe-CDs displayed good POD-like activity, and the doping of the Fe element greatly improved the catalytic activity of CDs, which can catalyze the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2 to produce a color signal. Besides, Fe-CDs maintained over 80 % of its initial activity even after 60 days, indicating its remarkable stability under the tested conditions (Fig. S1).

At the same time, the UV–vis absorption of five different polyphenols added to the Fe-CDs-H2O2-TMB system were studied (Fig. 2B). Under the same experimental conditions, the Fe-CDs-H2O2-TMB system had the lowest absorbance, and after the addition of polyphenols, the absorption peaks of the system all increased obviously. It is really intriguing, and this phenomenon is in contrast to nearly all the other nanozyme studies so far involving polyphenols, since the reductive reagents always induce a lower absorption response of the reaction system (Li et al., 2024b). The control experiment (Fig. S2) also excludes the potential effects caused by the impurities (such as Cu2+ or HPR, if present) in the polyphenols. The mechanism of this unexpected result will be discussed in the following sections. Here, one thing to note is that the enhancing effect of polyphenols on Fe-CDs activity is similar to that produced by natural enzyme cofactors (Villarino et al., 2020), so the polyphenols may play the role of cofactors for the Fe-CDs nanozyme in our system. Among them, EC had the strongest absorption peaks while K showed the weakest enhancement, indicating the varied cofactor ability of different polyphenols. The solution of the corresponding system also exhibited different shades of blue. Therefore, based on the unique enhancement effect on the reaction rate of the system with each polyphenol, it is feasible to construct a system for polyphenol detection and provide a basis for subsequent identification of polyphenols.

In order to further explore the POD-like property of Fe-CDs, the steady-state kinetic analysis was carried out through the record of absorbance at 650 nm under changing concentrations of TMB and H2O2 (Fig. S3). Within 20 min, the absorbance at 650 nm of the reaction system increased with the extension of time, and the reaction rate rose with the increase of TMB and H2O2 concentration. Adhering to the standard Michaelis-Menten model, the nanozyme parameters were obtained through the double reciprocal diagram of the substrate concentration and the reaction rate (Fig. 2C and D). The Michaelis constant (Km) and the maximum initial velocity (Vmax) of Fe-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) were calculated to be 0.697 mM and 18.32 × 10−7 M⋅s−1 for TMB, and 0.217 mM and 12.75 × 10−7 M⋅s−1 for H2O2, respectively. Compared with traditional horseradish peroxidase (HRP), the Km values of Fe-CDs are relatively smaller, indicating the good affinity of Fe-CDs to TMB and H2O2. Furthermore, the nanozyme parameters in the presence of polyphenols (EC) were also obtained (Fig. 2C and D), which were generally superior to those of Fe-CDs themselves, with at least a 2-fold increase in Vmax. This is a direct indication of the cofactor effect of polyphenols.

In addition, a kinetic investigation of the mechanism of the reaction of Fe-CDs with TMB and H2O2 was carried out. In bi-substrate enzyme reactions, it is acknowledged that the sequential and ping-pong mechanisms are the two main kinetic mechanisms. The reaction producing the non-parallel lines in the Lineweaver-Burk diagram follows a sequential mechanism, while the ping-pong mechanism results in parallel lines (Jian et al., 2022; Tan et al., 2024). Hence, Lineweaver-Burk plots of various TMB and H2O2 concentrations under three different H2O2/TMB dosages were constructed (Fig. 2E and F). It was seen clearly that there were three non-parallel lines in the Lineweaver-Burk plot of Fe-CDs, which had the characteristic pattern of sequential mechanism, indicating that H2O2 and TMB were first combined with Fe-CDs to form a ternary complex, and then the products of ox-TMB and H2O were released.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic studies of cofactor-enhanced POD-like activity of Fe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To study the catalytic mechanism of Fe-CDs, the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) intermediates (▪OH, ▪O2 −, and 1O2) was tested with various techniques. First, Electron spin resonance (ESR) measurements were performed by applying 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxide (TEMPO) as the separate capture reagent. As illustrated in Fig. 3 A, the 1:2:2:1 characteristic peak of DMPO/▪OH adduct, the 1:1:1 peak of TEMPO/1O2 adduct, and the 1:1:1:1 peak of DMPO/▪O2 − adduct were clearly observed, demonstrating the production of all three radicals of ▪OH, ▪O2 −, and 1O2. This result suggested that Fe-CDs catalyzed the decomposition of H2O2 to produce the three ROS radicals, which were the vital species to oxidize TMB. Second, the UV–vis characterization was also used to confirm the generation of ROS, with mannitol (Mnt), tryptophan (Trp), and p-benzoquinone (PBQ) selected as scavengers of ▪OH, 1O2 and ▪O2 −, respectively. When the radicals were consumed by the scavengers, the oxidation efficiency of TMB was reduced. As displayed in Fig. 3B, compared with the blank solution (Fe-CDs-H2O2-TMB), the absorbance at 650 nm after the addition of three scavengers all decreased, indicating the presence of ▪OH, 1O2, and ▪O2 − in the system, which was consistent with the result of ESR. To explore the function of the polyphenol cofactor, the ROS generation was also investigated in the presence of polyphenols, and EC was chosen as the representative. With the addition of EC, the scavengers had a similar reducing effect on the absorbance at 650 nm, but the whole intensity were higher than the systems without EC. Therefore, it was deduced that with the existence of EC, Fe-CDs still followed the similar catalytic procedure to produce ROS radicals, and more importantly, the amount of radical was obviously increased.

Finally, the fluorescence method was applied to further verify the results obtained by UV–vis characterization. When capturing ▪OH, terephthalic acid (TA) will transfer to fluorescent 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid (TAOH) with emission at around 430–445 nm. As shown in Fig. 3C, quite weak emission was observed in the presence of only TA, or TA + H2O2, while the emission peak increased obviously with the addition of Fe-CDs. What's more, the emission intensity soared nearly 2.5 times when EC was involved. This evidence convincingly showed that Fe-CDs catalyzed the production of ▪OH, and the EC cofactor greatly enhanced the catalytic ability of Fe-CDs to produce more radicals. Also, 9,10-anthracenediyl-bis (methylene) dimalonic acid (ABDA) was used to capture 1O2 and dihydroethidium (DHE) was used to capture ▪O2 −. The fluorescence of ABDA at 400–600 nm and DHE at 445 nm would be quenched upon binding with 1O2 and ▪O2 −, respectively. As can be seen in Fig. 3D and E, the conclusion was also obtained that the notable fluorescence quenching happened upon the existence of Fe-CDs, and the addition of EC further lowered the emission intensity. Overall, these results firmly indicated that Fe-CDs catalyzed the decomposition of H2O2 to produce ▪OH, 1O2, and ▪O2 −, which achieved the oxidation of TMB. The presence of EC greatly accelerated the catalytic process, playing the role of nanozyme cofactor.

After ascertaining the enhanced function of EC (or polyphenol) to Fe-CDs, the next mission is to dig out the underlying mechanism. At the start, the UV–vis absorption measurement of Fe-CDs, EC, and Fe-CDs with EC mixture were performed, respectively (Fig. S4). Compared with the separate Fe-CDs and EC, the apparent change of the characteristic peaks and the hypochromic effect in the curve of mixture solution implied that Fe-CDs and EC existed not in the free state, but interacted with each other to form a new complex when mixing together (Yan et al., 2024b). If EC formed complex with Fe-CDs, the surface structure of Fe-CDs would be changed, thereby affecting the optical properties of Fe-CDs. This was confirmed by the fluorescence investigation of Fe-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of EC. The fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs at 441 nm enhanced with increasing EC concentration (Fig. 3F), suggesting the interaction between Fe-CDs and EC. The negligible change of the average fluorescence lifetime of Fe-CDs before (6.501 ns) and after (6.822 ns) the addition of EC (Fig. 3G) demonstrated that the fluorescence enhancement process followed a static mechanism, i.e., Fe-CDs and EC formed a complex with a specific structure (Huang et al., 2015), which was consistent with the previous speculation. This mode was similar to that of the natural enzymes, since the natural enzymes need to bind to its cofactor to exert the biological effect (Hashim and Adnan, 1994; Wu et al., 2024).

Considering the surface composition of Fe-CDs, it is natural to deduce that the formation of the new complex between EC (or polyphenols) and Fe-CDs may occur through the hydrogen bonding between phenolic hydroxyl groups of EC and amino groups and other oxygen functional groups on the surface of Fe-CDs, together with the chelation of EC with dopped Fe ions on the surface of Fe-CDs. The obtained complex allows easy access and higher affinity of H2O2 to Fe-CDs (Pan et al., 2022), leading to enhanced catalytic activity (Fig. 3H). To explore whether the complex structure only exists in this system, namely, whether polyphenols can only be the cofactor of Fe-CDs nanozyme synthesized with our method, other nanozymes with different doping elements, or synthesized via different methods are also discussed. Initially, a batch of CDs nanozymes were prepared via our synthesis method, distinct only in terms of their elemental doping. As shown in Fig. 3I, the catalytic activity of CDs was almost negligible without metal element doping, and the addition of EC had no effect. Both Fe-CDs and Cu-CDs exhibited POD-like activity, which were enhanced by 193 % and 28 % in the addition of EC, respectively. Besides, the activity of CDs without S doping remained largely unchanged, and EC still had an increasing effect up to 90 %. Consequently, it was speculated that Fe or Cu doping is important for the CDs activity, and the chelation of EC with Fe or Cu might have great importance during the complex formation; S doping has little effect in this reaction system. Next, another batch of CDs with different carbon sources were prepared through a different method. These CDs, with or without Fe doping, showed the most common phenomenon that the addition of EC induced the reduction of absorbance. This indicated that not only metal doping, the surface condition of CDs also affected the interaction with EC. Lastly, the ZIF nanozyme, with structure and property far away from CDs, was also prepared for cofactor exploration. It was interesting to find that Fe-doped ZIF-8 exhibited the absorbance enhancement in presence of EC, while ZIF-8 without Fe-doping showed lower response. Such distinct results in different systems imply the nanozyme function process is quite complicated. A very preliminary explanation is given here and the systematic and deep research is needed to unveil the detailed mechanism of polyphenol enhancing.

Generally, the results above have indicated that if considered as a novel nanozyme cofactor, the formation of a new composite on the surface of Fe-CDs is necessary. Since the binding sites for polyphenols on the Fe-CDs surface are limited and polyphenols inherently possess strong reductive ability, the enhancement effect of polyphenols is inevitably influenced by the polyphenol concentration in the system. The kinetic study showed that the reaction rate of TMB oxidation in the presence of 50 μg/mL EC was much higher than that in the presence of 800 μg/mL and 2000 μg/mL of EC, and the reaction at 2000 μg/mL EC was obviously inhibited by certain factor (Fig. S5). This phenomenon was further explained by detailed concentration exploration. Fig. 3J illustrated that EC had the boosting effect on the nanozyme in the concentration range of about 0–1400 μg/mL, and the dampening effect when higher than 1500 μg/mL. The catalytic activity of Fe-CDs was maximal when EC reached 100 μg/mL, at which point all the possible sites on the Fe-CDs surface had been bound to polyphenols and became saturated. As a result, the continuous increase in EC concentration had no effect on the catalytic activity of Fe-CDs, but rather strengthened the reductivity of EC, which led to a gradual decrease in the overall absorbance of the system. When EC concentration was higher than 1500 μg/mL, the reductivity of EC finally surpassed the enhancement effect and played the dominant role. At this range, the addition of EC induced a decrease in absorbance, just as reported in most literature (Magiera et al., 2022; Rudrapal et al., 2024; Vidot et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognition of tea polyphenols by Fe-CDs-based sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: As discussed in Section 3.2, different polyphenols had varied enhancing effects on the Fe-CDs-H2O2-TMB system, and this makes the discrimination of different polyphenols possible based on the Fe-CDs constructed colorimetric sensor array. Since three peaks can be observed in the UV–vis absorption curve, the absorbance change (ΔA = A-A0, A and A0 represent the absorbance of the reaction system in the presence and absence of tea polyphenols, respectively) at 370 nm, 450 nm, and 650 nm as the sensing channels was developed for drawing the unique fingerprint of each polyphenol. Five polyphenols, with the molecule structure listed in Fig. S6, were selected as the representatives.

To obtain a clear discrimination response, the reaction conditions, including the concentrations of Fe-CDs, TMB and H2O2, and reaction pH, were studied to analyze their effect on the signal of the sensing channels. As expected in Fig. S7A, with the increasing of Fe-CDs concentration from 0.05 to 1 mg/mL and 50 μg/mL EC as the polyphenol example, the absorbance changes grew larger at three sensing channels (ΔA370, ΔA450, and ΔA650), due to the core function of Fe-CDs in the catalytic reaction. Given the catalytic efficiency and cost-effectiveness, 0.5 mg/mL Fe-CDs was chosen for the construction of the sensor array. The concentration ratio of TMB to H2O2 affected the initial reaction rate and the reaction progress (Liu et al., 2022b), so simply increasing either TMB or H2O2 did not result in a persistent elevation in absorbance (Fig. S7B and C). 5 mM TMB and 10 mM H2O2 were used in the following study, unless stated otherwise. Besides, the optimal reaction pH was found to be pH 4 (Fig. S7D), consistent with that of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and other reported POD-mimic nanozymes (Zhang et al., 2023; Zhu et al., 2024).

Based on the chosen condition, the absorbance signal of the sensing channels was identified and used to create a unique fingerprint for each polyphenol. In Fig. 4 A, the discrete ΔA370, ΔA450, and ΔA650 pattern of five different polyphenols at a concentration of 50 μg/mL was depicted, manifesting the clear differentiation among the five samples. Subsequently, 1, 10, 50, 70 and 100 μg/mL of various polyphenols were detected using this proposed sensor array. The canonical score plots in Fig. 4B–D, and Fig. S8A, B produced through PCA showed the five polyphenols at different concentrations were well clustered and completely distinguished from each other, demonstrating the adequate discrimination ability of the sensor array with a low concentration limit and a wide range. The distinct colorimetric patterns in Fig. 4E revealed that the POD-like activity of Fe-CDs was strongly enhanced by EC, while a weaker effect was observed for K and EGC. Besides, the expression was higher at 370 nm and lower at 450 nm and 650 nm, indicating that the ΔA370 channel had a greater impact on the discrimination of the five polyphenols with a stronger correlation. Besides the implication of the five polyphenols well clustered without misclassification, the HCA result in Fig. 4F further revealed the varied enhancing effect of them. The separate branch and the largest Euclidean distance of EC clearly illustrated that EC exhibited significant difference from other polyphenols, i.e., the most effective cofactor of Fe-CDs. Additionally, CG and GA showed a similar enhancing effect, while K and EGC had analogous reaction properties. However, the resemblance of enhancing effect was different from the resemblance of polyphenol structure, indicating that other parameters might also be involved in the complex formation on Fe-CDs surface.

In order to examine the usability and multiple detection capabilities of the sensor arrays in real complex systems, the binary (EC:EGC), ternary (EC:EGC:K), and quinary (EC:EGC:GA:CG:K) polyphenol mixtures with different ratios were further identified. As depicted in Fig. 4G–and H, the binary, and ternary mixtures were analyzed and could be distinguished very simply and quickly. It is noteworthy that 8 channels were used for the discrimination of the quinary mixtures (Fig. 4J), even more than those required for the identification of real tea (Fig. S9B–D), indicating that the complexity of the quinary mixtures we designed was close to or exceeded that of the real samples. The quinary mixtures were distinguished into groups without overlapping or interfering with each other (Fig. 4I), which demonstrated the excellent performance and great potential of the sensor array in identifying polyphenol mixtures, and laid a good foundation for the detection of real tea samples.

Moreover, the quantitative discrimination of polyphenols by the sensor array was also evaluated. The polyphenol EC was chosen as an example for analysis, and the difference in absorbance at the three channels varied with the increase in EC concentration (Fig. S9A). EC of different concentrations was successfully distinguished on the PCA score plot (Fig. 4K), and a good linear relationship was exhibited between Factor 1 and EC concentration in the range of 1–10 μg/mL (Fig. 4L). This result demonstrated that this nanozyme-based sensor array could be used to simultaneously identify and quantify the targeted polyphenols.

The contents and types of polyphenols in various teas are different, and they are strongly affected by the production process and storage time. The monitoring of tea polyphenols is crucial for the quality control of tea products. In view of the good differentiation ability of the sensor array, we further applied it to the identification of actual samples. Firstly, the famous dark tea of Guangxi, Liupao tea, was identified. The longer storage age of Liupao tea, the richer nutrients it contains and thus the higher economic value (Liang et al., 2025), so the analysis and identification of the vintage of Liupao tea is an important part of quality identification. The Liupao tea from Tianyu manufacturer with 10, 8, <1 storage year was noted as T10, T08 and T00, respectively, while Liupao tea from Maosheng with 15, 10, <1 storage year was separately noted as M15, M10 and M00. The six Liupao tea samples were accurately identified into six clusters, and T10 had the obvious difference from other samples (Fig. 5 A and B). Besides, it could be seen from the HCA dendrogram (Fig. 5C) that T10, T08 and T00 showed the expected change with the increasing of storage age, while M15, M10 and M00 seemed to have similar properties, just in terms of polyphenols.

Secondly, various types of tea with different degree of fermentation, namely Enshi Yulu, Liupao tea, Rose black tea, and Puer tea, were identified. The PCA analysis displayed the four types of tea were clearly differentiated (Fig. 5D–F), and the longest branch of Enshi Yulu in HCA plots suggested that the difference between Enshi Yulu tea and the group of Liupao tea, Rose black tea, and Puer tea were obvious, which was also related to the fact that the latter are fermented teas, whereas Enshi Yulu is a green tea (no fermentation) with a higher content of polyphenols. Finally, the discrimination of common packaged tea beverages (Fig. 5G and H) was further performed. Such commercial tea beverages tend to contain some food additives such as sugar, pigment, and antioxidants, and the composition is much more complex than that of brewed tea. As can be seen from Fig. 5I, Lemon tea, Black tea, Black oolong tea, and Ganpu tea were effectively classified into four groups, and the similarity among Black tea, Black oolong tea and Ganpu tea was higher. It might be ascribed to the more additives and less tea polyphenol in Lemon tea than other tea beverages. The above results demonstrated the feasibility of the constructed sensor array in the analysis of diverse tea products with fast and accurate identification and authentication.

In summary, a Fe-CDs-based colorimetric sensor array has been developed for the identification of various polyphenols and, furthermore, for the authentication of diverse tea products. Intriguingly, it is found that the addition of polyphenols elevates the classic TMB oxidation in nanozyme system, i.e., enhances the activity of Fe-CDs, which is contrast to the reported phenomenon. In analogy to natural enzymes, polyphenols play as a cofactor of Fe-CDs nanozyme. Through the mechanism exploration, it is proved that the presence of polyphenols produces more amount of ▪OH, ▪O2 −, and 1O2, accelerating the oxidation of TMB. This is ascribed to the formation of new complex on Fe-CDs with polyphenols. Even though the enhancement effect of polyphenols is newly discovered, it is not restricted to Fe-CDs system. Cu-CDs and even Fe/Cu-ZIF also have the enhanced activity upon the addition of polyphenols. Doping element, surface condition, and the polyphenol concentration are factors related to the overall effect. Each of the polyphenols exhibits unique colorimetric fingerprint and can be identified by PCA and HCA at concentration as low as l μg/mL. Besides, polyphenol mixtures with different molar ratios, the simulant of real samples, are also well distinguished. The differentiation of tea leaves of different years and categories, and various tea drinks prove the excellent identification ability and practical applicability of the sensor array. This study not only provides new inspiration for exploring and tuning the nanozyme activity, but develops a cost-effective sensing platform for the quality control of tea products.

Yanying Zheng: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wenju Yan: Data curation. Nansheng Li: Investigation. Jinfang Nie: Writing – review & editing. Yun Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Wenxin Niu: Data curation. Yali Yuan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.
